verdicts.question: What type of roof is present at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 10-14 show close-up and mid-range views of the roof with distinctly orange/red Mangalore clay interlocking tiles, including clear texture and ridge detail on pages 12-14.
verdicts.rationale: Multiple roof photos (pages 10-14) unambiguously show Mangalore-style clay tiles consistent with the designer's 'Tiled Roof / Mangalore Tiled Roof' answer.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstructions on the roof that could affect the solar installation?
verdicts.designer_answer: details: The obstruction on the roof is sloped roof that could affect the solar installation
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 (captioned 'Obstruction Details') shows the headroom glass-walled structure occupying part of the upper roof terrace and the sloped Mangalore tiled sections. No separate obstructions (AC units, water tanks, overhanging trees) are visible on the panel zones themselves.
verdicts.rationale: The Obstruction Details photos (page 8) document the headroom structure and sloped roof as the main installation challenge, consistent with the designer's description that the sloped roof itself is the primary obstruction.

verdicts.question: What is the angle of the panels? (in degrees)
verdicts.designer_notes: The existing roof is 23 degree slope
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 12-13 show the tiled roof in profile; the slope appears moderate (visually consistent with roughly 20-25 degrees). The tile overhang and rise-to-run ratio visible in the profile shots are typical of standard Mangalore-tiled residential roofs in Bangalore.
verdicts.rationale: Roof profile photos (pages 12-13) show a slope consistent with ~23 degrees; traditional Mangalore tile roofs in Bangalore commonly fall in the 20-25 degree range, and the visual tilt is not inconsistent with the stated value.

verdicts.question: In case of sloped roof, what is the slope angle of the roof? (in degrees)
verdicts.photo_observation: Same roof profile views as Q3 (pages 12-13); slope visually appears ~20-25 degrees.
verdicts.rationale: Consistent with Q3 assessment; the sloped tiled roof photographs support a ~23 degree slope angle as stated by the designer.

verdicts.question: On how many roofs at the site are we installing solar?
verdicts.designer_notes: The existing two roof are located in same building
verdicts.photo_observation: 3D top-view diagram (page 18) and side/east-view diagrams (pages 19-20) clearly show two distinct gable tiled roof sections with solar panels on both. Page 13 shows the ridge from above confirming two separate sloped planes meeting at the peak.
verdicts.rationale: The site layout 3D top-view (page 18) and elevation diagrams (pages 19-20) distinctly show two separate tiled roof sections on the same building, confirming the designer's answer of 2 roofs.

verdicts.question: If it is a Tiled roof, what is underneath the tiles?
verdicts.photo_observation: Close-up tile photos (pages 12-14) show only the surface of the Mangalore tiles; no photo reveals the substrate beneath. The building overall appears to be a modern residential structure, but the tile underside is not directly visible in any photograph.
verdicts.rationale: No photo shows what lies beneath the Mangalore tiles; cannot visually confirm RCC vs. wooden rafters or other substrate. Physical tile-lifting inspection required for confirmation.

verdicts.question: What type of mounting structure is planned?
verdicts.photo_observation: All roof photos show bare Mangalore tiles without any mounting hardware installed, as expected for a pre-installation site survey.
verdicts.rationale: This is a pre-installation survey; no mounting hardware is installed or visible. The planned mini-rail mounting type cannot be verified against site photos at this stage.

verdicts.question: What is the orientation of the solar panels?
verdicts.designer_notes: North side four panels and south side four panels
verdicts.photo_observation: The 3D top-view layout diagram (page 18) includes a north arrow/compass indicator. Panels are shown placed on two opposing slopes of the same gable roof ridge, with the ridge alignment consistent with an east-west ridge line producing north and south facing slopes.
verdicts.rationale: The 3D top-view diagram (page 18) includes a north indicator and shows panels on two opposing gable slopes; the ridge geometry is consistent with North- and South-facing panels as stated by the designer.

verdicts.question: How can the roof be accessed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 7 and 9 each independently show an interior staircase with marble/granite steps and a glass balustrade providing clear multi-floor access up to the roof level.
verdicts.rationale: Two separate photos (pages 7 and 9) clearly show a well-maintained interior marble staircase; consistent with 'Staircase' roof access answer.

verdicts.question: Is the building currently under construction?
verdicts.photo_observation: Building exterior photos (pages 5, 21) show a fully completed, painted residential villa with installed windows, finished floor tiling, intact glass railings, and a landscaped garden. No scaffolding, bare brick, or unfinished structures are visible anywhere.
verdicts.rationale: Multiple exterior and interior photos show a fully completed building with no construction activity or unfinished elements, confirming the building is not under construction.

verdicts.question: What is the height of the building and how many floors does it have?
verdicts.photo_observation: Building side view (page 6) shows ground floor plus two upper floors. The headroom structure is clearly visible on page 7 (bottom) as a separate small room at roof level with Mangalore tiles. Page 21 (building exterior front) shows the full height profile confirming G+2 plus the small upper enclosure.
verdicts.rationale: Side elevation (page 6) and building exterior (page 21) together confirm G+2 floors; the headroom room is distinctly visible in page 7 as a separate enclosure on the roof - fully consistent with G+2+Headroom at ~12m.

verdicts.question: Is the site photo matching with the building description?
verdicts.photo_observation: Building exterior photo (page 21) shows a residential villa with Mangalore tiled roof, multi-floor structure, and garden frontage - all matching the project description (Villa#514, residential, G+2+Headroom, Bangalore).
verdicts.rationale: The building exterior photograph (page 21) is fully consistent with all project descriptors: residential villa, tiled roof, multi-floor structure at a gated villa community in Bangalore.

verdicts.question: Is there an overhead HT line passing within a 10-meter radius of the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Multiple exterior photos (pages 5, 6, 15, 21) show the building surroundings including the access road, side alleyway, and adjacent properties. No thick high-tension wires, large HT transmission poles, or HT infrastructure are visible in any photo.
verdicts.rationale: No overhead HT wires or large transmission poles are visible in any of the exterior/surrounding photos; the setting is a residential villa colony with no visible HT infrastructure, consistent with 'No HT line within 10m'.

verdicts.question: Can the delivery van/truck reach the material unloading point?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 21 (building exterior) shows a car parked directly in front of the building on a paved driveway. Page 5 (top image) shows the building approach from a clear road with vehicle-accessible frontage.
verdicts.rationale: A vehicle is visibly parked at the entrance in the building exterior photo (page 21) and the driveway approach is shown on page 5; vehicular delivery access to the unloading point is clearly feasible.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstacles at the site that could hinder material lifting?
verdicts.photo_observation: Staircase photos (pages 7, 9) show clear marble stairs with intact glass railings and no visible obstructions, low beams, or tight corners on the lifting route. Page 5 (bottom) shows the side access path appearing unobstructed.
verdicts.rationale: Interior staircase photos (pages 7, 9) show unobstructed, well-maintained stairs with no visible impediments to material lifting; no tight corridors, low beams, or blocked passages are visible.

verdicts.question: Did you discuss the location of the earth pits with the customer?
verdicts.designer_answer: Yes, the location of the earth pits in garden area which is north facing
verdicts.photo_observation: Earth Pit Location photo (page 18, top) clearly shows a garden alleyway between the building and boundary wall with plants and soil, with overlay markers for 'DC Earth Pit' and 'LA Pit' placed in designated positions within the garden area.
verdicts.rationale: Page 18 explicitly labels the 'Earth Pit Location' with DC Earth Pit and LA Pit markers in a garden alley with visible plants and soil, consistent with the 'garden area' designation discussed with the customer.

verdicts.question: Where is the Internet Router located?
verdicts.photo_observation: No photo in the documentation shows the internet router or its location.
verdicts.rationale: 'Customer Scope' is a procedural designation; no router location photo is included and this item cannot be confirmed or contradicted through site photographs.

verdicts.question: What's the Wi-Fi strength of the router near to the String inverter/Envoy?
verdicts.photo_observation: No Wi-Fi signal measurement screenshot or device display showing signal strength is visible in any photo in the documentation.
verdicts.rationale: Wi-Fi signal strength is a measurement that cannot be verified visually from site photographs; no screenshot or measurement evidence is included in the documentation.

verdicts.question: Did you pin the site location in your WhatsApp when you were at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: No WhatsApp location pin screenshot is included in the photo documentation.
verdicts.rationale: WhatsApp location pinning is a procedural action that cannot be verified from site photos; no screenshot of the pin is present in the documentation.

verdicts.question: What type of energy meter is currently installed?
verdicts.designer_answer: Phase: Three Phase, Type: Digital, If 3-phase: Normal Three Phase
verdicts.photo_observation: Meter box photo (page 9, bottom) shows a panel with a meter on the left side displaying a green LED/digital indicator, consistent with a digital meter. The right section shows multiple MCB rows. The configuration is consistent with a digital three-phase installation.
verdicts.rationale: The meter box photo (page 9) shows a meter with a visible digital display (green LED/indicator) and a panel with multiple MCBs; consistent with a digital three-phase meter, though exact phase labeling is not legible at this photo resolution.

verdicts.question: Is the Meter cubicle photo matching with the previous answer?
verdicts.photo_observation: The 'Meter Box' section is clearly labeled and photographed on page 9 (bottom), showing an installed digital meter with visible LED display and MCB panel consistent with a digital installation.
verdicts.rationale: The Meter Box photo (page 9) is present, clearly labeled, and shows a digital meter consistent with the three-phase digital meter description in Q1.

verdicts.question: Has the termination point in the Main meter panel been identified?
verdicts.photo_observation: Equipment Location photo (page 17, bottom) shows a built-in cabinet with 'Main Meter' position explicitly labeled with a red overlay, confirming the termination point has been identified and documented.
verdicts.rationale: The Equipment Location photo (page 17) explicitly labels the 'Main Meter' position, confirming the termination point in the meter panel has been identified as part of the planned solar meter installation area.

verdicts.question: Is the Termination Point easily visible or sealed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Meter box photo (page 9) shows the panel with internal components visible and accessible - MCBs, wiring, and the meter face are all clearly visible in the open/accessible panel state.
verdicts.rationale: The meter box photo (page 9) shows the panel interior with accessible wiring and MCBs clearly visible; the open, accessible panel state is consistent with the termination point being 'Visible'.

verdicts.question: In case of an Enphase system, is the distance between the farthest micro-inverter and IQ Gateway > 50m?
verdicts.photo_observation: 3D site layout diagrams (pages 18-20) and building photos show a compact residential villa; both roof sections are on the same building. Total cable run from the rooftop panels to the ground-floor meter/IQ Gateway on a G+2 villa is well within 50m.
verdicts.rationale: The site layout diagrams (pages 18-20) show a compact single-building residential villa where all panels and the IQ Gateway would be co-located; the building dimensions are clearly consistent with a sub-50m micro-inverter-to-gateway run.

verdicts.question: In Main meter panel, is empty space available for solar meter?
verdicts.photo_observation: Equipment Location photo (page 17, bottom) explicitly shows a 'Solar Meter' position (cyan/blue overlay label) marked adjacent to the 'Main Meter' position within the cabinet, confirming dedicated space has been identified.
verdicts.rationale: The Equipment Location photo (page 17) directly and explicitly labels a 'Solar Meter' placement area next to the Main Meter, confirming available space for the solar meter.

verdicts.question: In Main meter panel, is empty space available to house the Enphase accessories or ACDB components?
verdicts.photo_observation: Meter box photo (page 9) shows a two-section panel with the right section containing MCBs and some additional space visible. Equipment Location photo (page 17) shows the cabinet with planned placement areas labeled.
verdicts.rationale: The meter box photo (page 9) and equipment location diagram (page 17) together show a panel with space in the right section and labeled planned placement areas, consistent with available space for Enphase accessories.

verdicts.question: Is there space available to dig DC, AC and LA earth pits?
verdicts.photo_observation: Earth Pit Location photo (page 18, top) shows a garden alleyway between the building and boundary wall with visible soil and plants, and overlay markers for 'DC Earth Pit' and 'LA Pit' placed in clearly accessible garden positions.
verdicts.rationale: The Earth Pit Location photo (page 18) shows a garden alleyway with visible soil area and explicit DC Earth Pit and LA Pit markers in designated positions; space for digging is clearly present.

verdicts.question: Are there any safety risks associated with accessing the roof for installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Staircase photos (pages 7, 9) show well-maintained marble stairs with intact glass railings and no visible hazards. Rooftop terrace photos (pages 10-11) show a clear, accessible area without loose tiles, exposed edges, or unstable surfaces.
verdicts.rationale: Staircase and rooftop photos show a well-maintained building with intact safety features; no cracked steps, broken railings, unstable surfaces, or other visible safety hazards are present.

verdicts.question: Is space present in rooftop to store the panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: Material Storage photo (page 10, top, captioned 'Material Storage') shows the rooftop terrace area around the headroom structure; the flat terrace adjacent to the headroom provides an open surface suitable for horizontally laying panels during installation staging.
verdicts.rationale: The rooftop terrace visible around the headroom structure (pages 10-11) provides flat, open space suitable for horizontal panel storage, consistent with the 'Horizontally sleeping' answer.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimensions for solar meter cubicle placement (L x W of wall).
verdicts.photo_observation: Equipment Location photo (page 17) shows the solar meter placement area within a built-in cabinet/utility space, but no measurement scale reference or measuring tape is visible to verify the stated 850x950mm wall dimensions.
verdicts.rationale: The solar meter placement area is photographed (page 17), but exact dimensions (850mm x 950mm) cannot be measured or verified from the photo without a scale reference; field measurement required for confirmation.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimension for earth pits location (L x W of available space).
verdicts.photo_observation: Earth Pit Location photo (page 18) shows the garden alleyway with earth pit markers, but no measurement tape or scale reference is present to verify the stated 1500x1500mm available space.
verdicts.rationale: The earth pit location is documented (page 18), but the exact dimensions (1500mm x 1500mm) cannot be verified from the photo without a scale reference; field measurement needed for confirmation.

verdicts.question: Is there any shadow that affects the solar installation?
verdicts.designer_notes: The existing building shadow will fall on the panels during second half of every month
verdicts.photo_observation: 3D shadow analysis diagrams (pages 19-20) model the building structure and show angular shading effects on the proposed roof sections. The Observations & Recommendations section (page 21, item 4) explicitly states: 'the customer has confirmed that shading on the panels is acceptable,' directly confirming shadow presence and customer acknowledgement.
verdicts.rationale: Shadow analysis 3D models (pages 19-20) visually demonstrate shading on the roof sections, and the Observations note on page 21 explicitly confirms shadow exists and has been accepted by the customer - fully consistent with the designer's 'Yes' answer.
